Pre-training We pre-train for 200k steps on 64 Google
Cloud TPUs, with a batch size of 512 and a learning rate
of 3e-5, using BERT’s default optimizer. The document
embedding step for the MIPS index is parallelized over 16
TPUs. For each example, we retrieve and marginalize over
8 candidate documents, including the null document ∅.
We experiment with two choices of the pre-training corpus
X : (1) Wikipedia, which is identical to the knowledge cor-
pus Z, and (2) CC-News, our reproduction of the corpus of
English news proposed by
Liu et al. (2019).
4.4. Main results
Table 1 shows the accuracy of different approaches on the
three Open-QA datasets. REALM outperform all previous
approaches by a signiﬁcant margin. Table
1 also shows the
number of parameters for each model.
As reported in the concurrent work of Roberts et al. (2020),
the generative Open-QA systems based on T5 are surpris-
ingly powerful, with the largest T5-11B model outperform-
ing the previous best Open-QA system. Increasing the size
of T5 yields consistent improvement, but comes at signif-
icant computational cost (from Base to 11B, the model is
50 times larger, and gains roughly 5 points in accuracy). In
contrast, REALM outperforms the largest T5-11B model
while being 30 times smaller. It is also important to note
that T5 accesses additional reading comprehension data
from SQuAD during its pre-training (100,000+ examples).
Access to such data could also beneﬁt REALM, but was not
used in our experiments.
Among all systems, the most direct comparison with
REALM is ORQA (
Lee et al., 2019), where the ﬁne-tuning
setup, hyperparameters and training data are identical. Th e
improvement of REALM over ORQA is purely due to bet-
ter pre-training methods. The results also indicate that ou r
method of pre-training can be applied both on (1) the single-
corpus setting ( X = Wikipedia, Z = Wikipedia), or (2) the
separate-corpus setting ( X = CC-News, Z = Wikipedia).
Compared to other retrieval-based systems (
Asai et al.,
2019; Min et al. , 2019a;b) which often retrieve from 20 to
80 documents, our system gets the overall best performance
while only retrieving 5 documents.

questions.baseline_answer:
The REALM (Retrieval-Augmented Language Model) retrieves from a textual knowledge source or corpus, which is explicitly accessed by a learned textual knowledge retriever incorporated into the pre-training algorithm. Instead of storing knowledge solely in the model parameters, REALM explicitly uses this external knowledge retriever to access world knowledge during training and inference. This approach exposes the role of world knowledge to the model by asking it to retrieve relevant textual information, which it then uses to augment language model pre-training and improve its understanding and generation capabilities [1][2][3][4][5].

Citations:  
[1][2][3][4][5] - data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunks 84.0, 85.0, 96.0, 120.0, and 115.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence notes consistently state that REALM enhances language model pre-training by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Unlike models that store knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly leverages world knowledge by retrieving relevant texts from an external source during training [E1][E2][E3]. However, the evidence does not specify the exact external knowledge source or corpus from which REALM retrieves information [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Additionally, the notes do not detail how the retrieved knowledge is used by the model beyond this general retrieval role [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Therefore, information about the precise external knowledge source or corpus and the detailed usage of the retrieved knowledge by REALM is missing from the supplied evidence.

This top-5 retrieval accuracy metric is used to isolate the contribution of improving the retriever component during pre-training, separate from the effects of fine-tuning [E6][E8]. However, the exact downstream tasks or benchmarks used for the end-to-end evaluation are not specified in the provided evidence notes, so details on which specific benchmarks REALM is evaluated on are missing [E6][E9].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM stands for Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training. It is a novel framework that enhances language model pre-training algorithms by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever. Unlike traditional language models that store knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly incorporates world knowledge by enabling the model to access and retrieve relevant textual information during pre-training. This approach exposes the role of external knowledge more directly, augmenting the model’s understanding by leveraging retrieved text rather than relying exclusively on internal parameter storage of knowledge [1][2][3][4][5].

Citations:  
[1][2][3][4][5] data/2002.08909v1.pdf
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM, or Retrieval-Augmented Language Model, is a pre-training method that enhances language model pre-training algorithms by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever [E1]. Unlike traditional models that store knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge through retrieval mechanisms integrated into the training process [E2]. Specifically, during pre-training, REALM prompts the model to retrieve relevant textual information from an external source, thereby integrating external knowledge instead of embedding all knowledge internally [E3]. This retrieval-augmented language model pre-training framework augments standard language model training by requiring the model to access and use external knowledge dynamically [E4][E5].

arXiv:2002.08909v1 [cs.CL] 10 Feb 2020
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
Kelvin Guu * 1 Kenton Lee * 1 Zora T ung1 Panupong Pasupat 1 Ming-Wei Chang 1
Abstract
Language model pre-training has been shown to
capture a surprising amount of world knowledge,
crucial for NLP tasks such as question answer-
ing. However, this knowledge is stored implic-
itly in the parameters of a neural network, requir-
ing ever-larger networks to cover more facts. To
capture knowledge in a more modular and inter-
pretable way, we augment language model pre-
training with a latent knowledge retriever, which
allows the model to retrieve and attend over doc-
uments from a large corpus such as Wikipedia,
used during pre-training, ﬁne-tuning and infer-

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT in the way knowledge is stored and accessed. While models like BERT store knowledge directly in their parameters, REALM augments language model pre-training algorithms with a learned textual knowledge retriever. This means that instead of embedding all knowledge within the model's parameters, REALM explicitly exposes and accesses world knowledge via an external retrieval mechanism, allowing it to retrieve relevant textual information dynamically during inference rather than relying solely on stored parameter knowledge [1][2][3][4][5].

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT in where and how knowledge is stored and accessed by augmenting the language model pre-training with a learned textual knowledge retriever, instead of embedding all knowledge implicitly within the model parameters [E15][E16]. While traditional language models like BERT store knowledge implicitly inside their neural network parameters—requiring increasingly large models to cover more facts—REALM explicitly exposes and accesses world knowledge by retrieving relevant information from a large external document corpus, such as Wikipedia, during pre-training, fine-tuning, and inference [E17][E19]. This retrieval-augmented design allows REALM to attend to and incorporate external documents directly, making knowledge access more modular and interpretable compared to the implicit parameter storage in models like BERT [E17][E18]. Additionally, REALM’s knowledge retriever is trained in an unsupervised manner using masked language modeling with backpropagation through the retrieval step, enabling it to efficiently leverage millions of documents as part of its knowledge source [E17].

What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-

ing objective encourages meaningful retrievals. Here, we
show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-
curacy.
For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is
the “relevance score” that the knowledge retriever assigns
to document z. We can see how a single step of gradient
descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).

be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-

high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity. By jointly learning the re-
triever, REALM has the capacity to depend on information
beyond lexical overlap.
Scalable grounded neural memory The document in-
dex can be viewed as a memory where the keys are
the document embeddings. From this view, our work
share motivations with works such as product key mem-
ory (
Lample et al., 2019), which enables sub-linear mem-
ory access in a memory network ( Weston et al., 2014;
Graves et al., 2014; Sukhbaatar et al., 2015), allowing
these scalable memory layers to be integrated into large
language models. One main difference is that our memo-
ries are grounded—each memory is associated with a docu-
ment rather than unnamed value vectors. This level of inter-
pretability is crucial for applications like Open-QA, wher e
users would require provenance for a predicted answer to
be trustworthy.

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-

Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.

better explain the variance in the input text and en-

a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with

REALM's retrieve-then-predict framework works by decomposing the task of generating an output \( y \) given an input \( x \) into two steps involving a latent document variable \( z \) drawn from a knowledge corpus \( Z \). Specifically, given \( x \) (which could be a masked sentence in pre-training or a question in fine-tuning), the model first retrieves potentially relevant documents \( z \) from the corpus using a distribution \( p(z|x) \). This is the "retrieve" step.

The framework then conditions on both the input \( x \) and the retrieved document(s) \( z \) to predict the output \( y \), modeling this conditional distribution as \( p(y|z,x) \). Since the document \( z \) is latent (not directly observed), the overall likelihood of predicting \( y \) is obtained by marginalizing over all possible documents \( z \) in the corpus:

\[
p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} p(y|z,x) p(z|x).
\]

Thus, \( z \) serves as a latent variable representing knowledge residing in documents, which the model can attend to when generating \( y \) [1].

\[
p(z|x) = \frac{\exp f(x,z)}{\sum_{z'} \exp f(x,z')}, \quad f(x,z) = \text{Embed}_{input}(x)^\top \text{Embed}_{doc}(z),
\]

where both embedding functions are implemented with BERT-style Transformers that project inputs and documents into a suitable embedding space [1].

This framework explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by asking the model to retrieve relevant external documents rather than storing all knowledge solely within its parameters [E2]. However, the provided evidence does not explicitly describe the retrieved document as a latent variable \( z \), nor detail how the model conditions on such a latent variable to generate predictions [E3][E4][E5]. Therefore, the specific role of the latent document variable \( z \) within REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework is not supported by the available evidence.

What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-

show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-

For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is

descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected

from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

questions.baseline_answer:
The paper trains the retriever when the retrieved document is latent by optimizing the retriever to reward retrievals that improve the prediction accuracy of the language model. Specifically, for a query \(x\) and document \(z\), the retriever assigns a relevance score \(f(x, z)\) to each document. During training, the gradient of the log-likelihood of the correct output \(y\) with respect to the retriever parameters \(\theta\) involves a term \(r(z)\) defined as:

\[
r(z) = \left[\frac{p(y | z, x)}{p(y | x)} - 1 \right] p(z | x)
\]

This gradient encourages the retriever to increase the score for documents \(z\) that yield a higher probability of correctly predicting \(y\) than the expected probability over all documents, and decrease it otherwise. Hence, learning signals flow back to the retriever based on how much each retrieved document helps improve the prediction, allowing the retriever to learn which documents to prioritize despite the retrieval being latent (i.e., not supervised explicitly) [3].

Additional techniques such as salient span masking focus training on tokens requiring world knowledge, and modeling with a null document accounts for cases where retrieval is not useful. This combination of gradient-based updates to the retriever and those inductive biases guides the retriever toward meaningful document selection during pre-training [3].

questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence does not contain detailed information about how the paper trains the retriever when the retrieved document is latent, nor does it explain how the learning signal flows back to retrieval. Some sources mention improving the retriever during pre-training [E3][E4], but they do not describe the mechanisms or the training procedure related to latent document retrieval or gradient flow. Sources E1 and E2 are fragmentary and do not add clarity on this point [E1][E2]. Therefore, additional text or evidence is needed to fully answer this question.

4.4. Main results
Table 1 shows the accuracy of different approaches on the
three Open-QA datasets. REALM outperform all previous
approaches by a signiﬁcant margin. Table
1 also shows the
number of parameters for each model.
As reported in the concurrent work of Roberts et al. (2020),
the generative Open-QA systems based on T5 are surpris-
ingly powerful, with the largest T5-11B model outperform-
ing the previous best Open-QA system. Increasing the size
of T5 yields consistent improvement, but comes at signif-
icant computational cost (from Base to 11B, the model is
50 times larger, and gains roughly 5 points in accuracy). In
contrast, REALM outperforms the largest T5-11B model
while being 30 times smaller. It is also important to note
that T5 accesses additional reading comprehension data
from SQuAD during its pre-training (100,000+ examples).
Access to such data could also beneﬁt REALM, but was not
used in our experiments.
Among all systems, the most direct comparison with
REALM is ORQA (
Lee et al., 2019), where the ﬁne-tuning
setup, hyperparameters and training data are identical. Th e
improvement of REALM over ORQA is purely due to bet-
ter pre-training methods. The results also indicate that ou r
method of pre-training can be applied both on (1) the single-
corpus setting ( X = Wikipedia, Z = Wikipedia), or (2) the
separate-corpus setting ( X = CC-News, Z = Wikipedia).
Compared to other retrieval-based systems (
Asai et al.,
2019; Min et al. , 2019a;b) which often retrieve from 20 to
80 documents, our system gets the overall best performance
while only retrieving 5 documents.

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM makes retrieval over a large corpus practical during training and inference by employing several approximations and engineering choices:

1. **Cached and Asynchronously Updated Computation**: The retriever's computation for each document is designed to be cached and asynchronously updated. This allows for efficient handling of the large corpus by avoiding repeated computation for static documents during training [2].

2. **Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)**: Document selection is formulated as a Maximum Inner Product Search problem. This enables efficient retrieval by rapidly finding the documents whose embeddings have the highest similarity to the query representation, which is essential for scaling to millions of documents [2].

3. **Limited Number of Retrieved Documents**: Unlike other systems retrieving from a large number of documents (20 to 80), REALM only retrieves 5 candidate documents for each example, reducing the retrieval overhead while still achieving superior performance [1].

4. **Marginalization Over Candidates Including a Null Document**: For each training example, REALM retrieves and marginalizes over 8 candidate documents, including the null document ∅. This helps in efficiently optimizing the retrieval step as a latent variable that contributes to language modeling, without exhaustive search over the entire corpus [1].

5. **Parallelization Across TPUs**: The document embedding step for the MIPS index is parallelized over 16 TPUs, enabling scalable computation of document embeddings during pre-training [1].

Together, these techniques allow REALM to handle large-scale retrieval involving millions of documents efficiently during both pre-training and inference, with the entire model runnable on a single machine with a 12GB GPU [1][2].

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.
Examples of retrieved documents Table
3 shows an
example of the REALM masked language model predic-
tion. In this example, “ Fermat” is the correct word, and
REALM (row (c)) gives the word a much high probability
compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM
manages to retrieve some documents with a related fact
(row (b)), the marginalized probability of the correct an-
swer dramatically increases. This shows that REALM is
able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even
though it is trained with unsupervised text only.
5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

4. Experiments
We now evaluate our approach on the Open-QA task. In
this section, we describe in detail the benchmarks used and
the different approaches to which we compare empirically.
4.1. Open-QA Benchmarks
A number of benchmarks have been proposed for Open-
QA. In this work, we focus on datasets where the ques-
tion writers did not already know the answer. This yields
questions that reﬂect more realistic information-seeking
needs, and also avoids artifacts that can arise if the ques-
tion is formulated with a particular answer in mind. A
deeper justiﬁcation is given in
Lee et al. (2019). In all
cases, the predicted answer is evaluated via exact match
with any reference answer, following previous Open-QA
work (
Chen et al., 2017).
NaturalQuestions-Open The NaturalQuestions dataset
(Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) consists of naturally occurring
Google queries and their answers. Each answer also comes
with an “answer type”: following Lee et al. (2019), we only
keep questions that are categorized as “short answer type”
with at most ﬁve tokens. The dataset also provides a sug-
gested Wikipedia document to retrieve; like all models we
compare against, we do not provide this to our model.
WebQuestions The WebQuestions dataset (
Berant et al. ,
2013) was collected from the Google Suggest API, using
one seed question and expanding the set to related ques-
tions. We follow the setting deﬁned by
Chen et al. (2017).
CuratedTrec The CuratedTrec dataset is a collection of
question-answer pairs drawn from real user queries issued
on sites such as MSNSearch and AskJeeves. To account for
multiple correct answers or different spelling variations, the
answers in this dataset are deﬁned as regular expressions
that match all correct answers. It is unclear how to train
generation-based models with this type of supervision, so
we do not evaluate them on this dataset.
4.2. Approaches compared
Retrieval-based Open-QA Most existing Open-QA sys-
tems answer the input question by ﬁrst retrieving poten-
tially relevant documents from a knowledge corpus, and
then using a reading comprehension system to extract an
answer from the documents. In this paradigm, the knowl-
edge is stored explicitly in the corpus. We wish to compare
different methods for implementing retrieval.
Many approaches use non-learned heuristic retrieval such
as sparse bag-of-words matching (
Robertson et al. , 2009)
or entity linking on the question to select a small set of rel-
evant documents (e.g., 20). These documents are typically
then re-ranked using a learned model, but coverage may be
limited by the initial heuristic retrieval step. Approache s
such as DrQA (
Chen et al., 2017), HardEM ( Min et al. ,
2019a), GraphRetriever ( Min et al. , 2019b), and PathRe-
triever (Asai et al., 2019) in Table 1 are in this category.
Some recent approaches have proposed to implement learn-
able retrieval using a MIPS index. ORQA (
Lee et al. , 2019)
formulates Open-QA using a similar latent variable model
as REALM, and also trains by maximizing the marginal
likelihood. However, REALM adds a novel language
model pre-training step, and backpropagates into the MIPS
index, rather than using a ﬁxed index. In Table
1, we di-
rectly compare the two. It is also important to note that
the retrievers for both REALM pretraining and ORQA are
initialized using the Inverse Cloze Task, described in Sec-
tion
3.4.
Generation-based Open-QA An emerging alternative
approach to Open-QA is to model it as a sequence pre-
diction task: simply encode the question, and then decode
the answer token-by-token based on the encoding. While
it was initially unclear how large amounts of knowledge
could be injected into the model, GPT-2 (
Radford et al.,
2019) hinted at the possibility of directly generating an-
swers without using any given context via sequence-to-
sequence. However, their performance was not competi-
tive possibly due to the lack of ﬁne-tuning.

questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM significantly outperforms ORQA and other existing open-domain QA baselines on multiple benchmarks, as shown by experimental results [E11]. Specifically, on the NaturalQuestions-Open (NQ) dataset, REALM achieves an exact match accuracy of 39.2% when trained on Wikipedia, compared to ORQA’s 33.3%, with both models having similar parameter sizes of about 330 million [E11]. Similarly, REALM surpasses ORQA on WebQuestions (WQ) and CuratedTREC (CT) benchmarks [E11].

The improvement over ORQA is attributed primarily to REALM’s better pre-training scheme rather than differences in model size or the number of retrieved documents, since both systems retrieve five documents; REALM uses a novel end-to-end pre-training approach that dynamically updates the retrieval index [E11][E1]. Unlike ORQA, which employs a fixed MIPS retrieval index, REALM backpropagates through the MIPS index, enabling the retriever and encoder to co-adapt during training [E1][E10]. This joint optimization strengthens both the retriever and encoder components, as shown by performance drops occurring when either is reset to its baseline during ablation studies [E10].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM's retrieval-based approach contrasts with models that store knowledge only in their parameters by explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge. Instead of embedding all knowledge implicitly within the model's parameters, REALM augments language model pre-training with a learned textual knowledge retriever, making the knowledge more interpretable and modular. This approach allows the model to access external information dynamically during training or inference rather than relying solely on fixed, internalized knowledge in the model weights [1][2][3][4][5].

Instead of embedding all knowledge implicitly within the model’s parameters, REALM asks the model to access and utilize external knowledge through a learned retrieval mechanism [E2][E4]. This method contrasts with traditional parametric models by making the knowledge retrieval step explicit, thereby separating the stored information from the model’s parameters [E4]. The evidence notes consistently emphasize this explicit exposure of world knowledge as the key distinction, although no specific quantitative comparisons or detailed measurements are provided in the sources [E2][E4].

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

questions.baseline_answer:
The ablation or diagnostic evidence showing that retrieval pre-training improves REALM rather than only the reader component comes from reporting how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before applying any fine-tuning. This retrieval metric more significantly isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training. Thus, by examining the frequency of retrieving the correct answer prior to reader fine-tuning, the authors isolate and demonstrate the effect of the retrieval pre-training on REALM's retriever component itself, beyond improvements to the reader [1][2][4][5].

questions.orchestrator_answer: The REALM approach augments language model pre-training by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever, distinguishing it from models that store knowledge solely within their parameters [E1][E2]. This method explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by requiring the model to access retrieved external information during pre-training, rather than relying only on internal parameter embeddings [E3][E4]. However, the provided evidence notes do not include specific ablation or diagnostic experiments that isolate the contribution of retrieval pre-training from the reader component [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Therefore, based on the reviewed sources, direct ablation or diagnostic evidence demonstrating that retrieval pre-training itself—distinct from improvements in the reader component—leads to better performance is not available [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5].

4. Experiments
We now evaluate our approach on the Open-QA task. In
this section, we describe in detail the benchmarks used and
the different approaches to which we compare empirically.
4.1. Open-QA Benchmarks
A number of benchmarks have been proposed for Open-
QA. In this work, we focus on datasets where the ques-
tion writers did not already know the answer. This yields
questions that reﬂect more realistic information-seeking
needs, and also avoids artifacts that can arise if the ques-
tion is formulated with a particular answer in mind. A
deeper justiﬁcation is given in
Lee et al. (2019). In all
cases, the predicted answer is evaluated via exact match
with any reference answer, following previous Open-QA
work (
Chen et al., 2017).
NaturalQuestions-Open The NaturalQuestions dataset
(Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) consists of naturally occurring
Google queries and their answers. Each answer also comes
with an “answer type”: following Lee et al. (2019), we only
keep questions that are categorized as “short answer type”
with at most ﬁve tokens. The dataset also provides a sug-
gested Wikipedia document to retrieve; like all models we
compare against, we do not provide this to our model.
WebQuestions The WebQuestions dataset (
Berant et al. ,
2013) was collected from the Google Suggest API, using
one seed question and expanding the set to related ques-
tions. We follow the setting deﬁned by
Chen et al. (2017).
CuratedTrec The CuratedTrec dataset is a collection of
question-answer pairs drawn from real user queries issued
on sites such as MSNSearch and AskJeeves. To account for
multiple correct answers or different spelling variations, the
answers in this dataset are deﬁned as regular expressions
that match all correct answers. It is unclear how to train
generation-based models with this type of supervision, so
we do not evaluate them on this dataset.
4.2. Approaches compared
Retrieval-based Open-QA Most existing Open-QA sys-
tems answer the input question by ﬁrst retrieving poten-
tially relevant documents from a knowledge corpus, and
then using a reading comprehension system to extract an
answer from the documents. In this paradigm, the knowl-
edge is stored explicitly in the corpus. We wish to compare
different methods for implementing retrieval.
Many approaches use non-learned heuristic retrieval such
as sparse bag-of-words matching (
Robertson et al. , 2009)
or entity linking on the question to select a small set of rel-
evant documents (e.g., 20). These documents are typically
then re-ranked using a learned model, but coverage may be
limited by the initial heuristic retrieval step. Approache s
such as DrQA (
Chen et al., 2017), HardEM ( Min et al. ,
2019a), GraphRetriever ( Min et al. , 2019b), and PathRe-
triever (Asai et al., 2019) in Table 1 are in this category.
Some recent approaches have proposed to implement learn-
able retrieval using a MIPS index. ORQA (
Lee et al. , 2019)
formulates Open-QA using a similar latent variable model
as REALM, and also trains by maximizing the marginal
likelihood. However, REALM adds a novel language
model pre-training step, and backpropagates into the MIPS
index, rather than using a ﬁxed index. In Table
1, we di-
rectly compare the two. It is also important to note that
the retrievers for both REALM pretraining and ORQA are
initialized using the Inverse Cloze Task, described in Sec-
tion
3.4.
Generation-based Open-QA An emerging alternative
approach to Open-QA is to model it as a sequence pre-
diction task: simply encode the question, and then decode
the answer token-by-token based on the encoding. While
it was initially unclear how large amounts of knowledge
could be injected into the model, GPT-2 (
Radford et al.,
2019) hinted at the possibility of directly generating an-
swers without using any given context via sequence-to-
sequence. However, their performance was not competi-
tive possibly due to the lack of ﬁne-tuning.

questions.baseline_answer:
The evidence for why REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance can be synthesized from the ablation results on NaturalQuestions-Open noted in the context. REALM's effectiveness is demonstrated through several interconnected factors:

1. **Retrieval Quality**: The analysis reports how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals prior to any fine-tuning. This metric isolates the impact of the retriever's quality as improved during pre-training. The fact that this is highlighted suggests that REALM’s retriever is more capable of fetching relevant documents containing the correct answer compared to prior methods, thereby enhancing QA performance directly through better retrieval results [1][2][3][4][5].

2. **Pre-Training Contributions**: The improvement in retrieval performance before fine-tuning indicates that REALM’s pre-training procedure effectively improves the retriever. This pre-training helps the model learn representations that facilitate locating the gold answers in retrieved documents even without task-specific fine-tuning. Thus, pre-training critically enhances both retrieval and downstream QA effectiveness [1][2][3][4][5].

3. **Downstream Fine-Tuning**: Beyond pre-training improvements, the context emphasizes end-to-end results after applying fine-tuning, implying that the full REALM pipeline—retrieval followed by reading and answer extraction—is optimized during task-specific training. This fine-tuning step refines the model’s ability to integrate retrieved information into accurate answers, further boosting performance in open-domain QA [1][2][3][4][5].

In summary, the key contributions to improved open-domain QA performance by REALM come from enhancing retrieval quality during pre-training, supported by a robust retriever that significantly increases the incidence of gold answers in top retrievals, and fine-tuning that optimizes end-to-end answer extraction.

REALM improves open-domain question answering (Open-QA) performance by integrating a learned retriever that selects informative documents from a large corpus such as Wikipedia to aid the language model’s predictions [E1]. This retriever is trained end-to-end together with the language model using a performance-based signal derived from unsupervised text, which rewards retrievals that reduce the language model’s perplexity [E1]. Specifically, the retriever optimizes the marginal likelihood within a latent variable language model framework, effectively selecting documents that help predict correct answers [E1].

To manage the vast size of the candidate document corpus—often millions of documents—REALM employs computational strategies including caching, asynchronous retriever updates, and Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) to enable efficient retrieval [E1]. This approach contrasts with prior retrieval methods that rely on fixed indices or heuristic retrieval approaches, as REALM’s retriever is learnable and continuously updated through backpropagation, including into the MIPS index [E2].

During pre-training, improvements in the retriever are isolated by measuring how frequently the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning, demonstrating that REALM’s retrieval quality is significantly enhanced prior to downstream task adaptation [E3][E4][E5]. Finally, REALM is fine-tuned on Open-QA benchmarks such as NATURAL QUESTIONS-OPEN, WEB QUESTIONS, and CURATED TREC, achieving 4–16% absolute accuracy improvements over previous state-of-the-art systems, thus showing that retrieval quality, pre-training, and fine-tuning together contribute to substantial downstream Open-QA performance gains [E1].

REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing

compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.
2. Background
Language model pre-training The goal of language
model pre-training is to learn useful representations of la n-
guage, usually from unlabeled text corpora. The resulting
pre-trained model can then be further trained ( ﬁne-tuned)
for a downstream task of primary interest (in our case,
Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (
Dai & Le, 2015; Radford et al.

und
Language model pre-training The goal of language
model pre-training is to learn useful representations of la n-
guage, usually from unlabeled text corpora. The resulting
pre-trained model can then be further trained ( ﬁne-tuned)
for a downstream task of primary interest (in our case,
Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (
Dai & Le, 2015; Radford et al. , 2019).
We focus on the masked language model 2 (MLM) variant
of pre-training popularized by BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018).
In its basic form, an MLM is trained to predict the miss-
ing tokens in an input text passage. Given an unlabeled
pre-training corpus X (e.g., Wikipedia text), a training ex-
ample (x, y) can be generated by randomly masking to-
kens in a sampled piece of text (e.g., x = “The [MASK]
is the currency [MASK] the UK ”; y = (“pound”,
“of”)). The model uses its representation of the masked
input x to predict the token that should go in each mask.
A good MLM must learn to encode syntactic and semantic
information (e.g., to predict “ of”) as well as some world
knowledge (e.g., to predict “ pound”).
Open-domain question answering (Open-QA) To mea-
sure a model’s ability to incorporate world knowledge, we
need a downstream task where world knowledge is criti-
cal. Perhaps one of the most knowledge-intensive tasks in
natural language processing is open-domain question an-
swering (Open-QA): given a question x such as “ What is
the currency of the UK? ”, a model must output the
correct answer string y, “pound”. The “open” part of Open-
QA refers to the fact that the model does not receive a pre-
identiﬁed document that is known to contain the answer,
unlike traditional reading comprehension (RC) tasks such
as SQuAD (
Rajpurkar et al., 2016; 2018). While RC mod-
1We use the term “document” loosely to refer to a passage
from the knowledge corpus, not necessarily a whole article.
2Strictly speaking, MLM is not a standard language model,
since it does not deﬁne a distribution over the entire sequen ce
of tokens. In the paper we sometimes abuse the term “language
model” slightly to make the phrase shorter.

[Page 6]
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
4. Experiments
We now evaluate our approach on the Open-QA task. In
this section, we describe in detail the benchmarks used and
the different approaches to which we compare empirically.
4.1. Open-QA Benchmarks
A number of benchmarks have been proposed for Open-
QA. In this work, we focus on datasets where the ques-
tion writers did not already know the answer. This yields
questions that reﬂect more realistic information-seeking
needs, and also avoids artifacts that can arise if the ques-
tion is formulated with a particular answer in mind. A
deeper justiﬁcation is given in
Lee et al. (2019). In all
cases, the predicted answer is evaluated via exact match
with any reference answer, following previous Open-QA
work (
Chen et al., 2017).
NaturalQuestions-Open The NaturalQuestions dataset
(Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) consists of naturally occurring
Google queries and their answers. Each answer also comes
with an “answer type”: following Lee et al. (2019), we only
keep questions that are categorized as “short answer type”
with at most ﬁve tokens. The dataset also provides a sug-
gested Wikipedia document to retrieve; like all models we
compare against, we do not provide this to our model.
WebQuestions The WebQuestions dataset (
Berant et al. ,
2013) was collected from the Google Suggest API, using
one seed question and expanding the set to related ques-
tions. We follow the setting deﬁned by
Chen et al. (2017).
CuratedTrec The CuratedTrec dataset is a collection of
question-answer pairs drawn from real user queries issued
on sites such as MSNSearch and AskJeeves. To account for
multiple correct answers or different spelling variations, the
answers in this dataset are deﬁned as regular expressions
that match all correct answers. It is unclear how to train
generation-based models with this type of supervision, so
we do not evaluate them on this dataset.
4.2. Approaches compared
Retrieval-based Open-QA Most existing Open-QA sys-
tems answer the input question by ﬁrst retrieving poten-
tially relevant documents from a knowledge corpus, and
then using a reading comprehension system to extract an
answer from the documents. In this paradigm, the knowl-
edge is stored explicitly in the corpus. We wish to compare
different methods for implementing retrieval.
Many approaches use non-learned heuristic retrieval such
as sparse bag-of-words matching (
Robertson et al. , 2009)
or entity linking on the question to select a small set of rel-
evant documents (e.g., 20). These documents are typically
then re-ranked using a learned model, but coverage may be
limited by the initial heuristic retrieval step. Approache s
such as DrQA (
Chen et al., 2017), HardEM ( Min et al. ,
2019a), GraphRetriever ( Min et al. , 2019b), and PathRe-
triever (Asai et al., 2019) in Table 1 are in this category.
Some recent approaches have proposed to implement learn-
able retrieval using a MIPS index. ORQA (
Lee et al. , 2019)
formulates Open-QA using a similar latent variable model
as REALM, and also trains by maximizing the marginal
likelihood. However, REALM adds a novel language
model pre-training step, and backpropagates into the MIPS
index, rather than using a ﬁxed index. In Table
1, we di-
rectly compare the two. It is also important to note that
the retrievers for both REALM pretraining and ORQA are
initialized using the Inverse Cloze Task, described in Sec-
tion
3.4.
Generation-based Open-QA An emerging alternative
approach to Open-QA is to model it as a sequence pre-
diction task: simply encode the question, and then decode
the answer token-by-token based on the encoding. While
it was initially unclear how large amounts of knowledge
could be injected into the model, GPT-2 (
Radford et al.,
2019) hinted at the possibility of directly generating an-
swers without using any given context via sequence-to-
sequence. However, their performance was not competi-
tive possibly due to the lack of ﬁne-tuning.

[Page 3]
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =

The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (

Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

questions.baseline_answer:
A masked-language-model (MLM) pre-training objective can improve downstream open-domain question answering (Open-QA) in REALM through the following steps:

1. **Masked Language Modeling Pre-Training**: The MLM pre-training task involves randomly masking tokens in a sentence from a large unlabeled text corpus (like Wikipedia) and training the model to predict the masked tokens based on the surrounding context. This step helps the model learn rich syntactic, semantic, and world knowledge representations [3].

2. **Incorporating Retrieval During Pre-Training**: REALM extends the MLM approach by integrating a retrieval component that retrieves relevant documents from a large knowledge corpus before predicting the masked tokens. For each input with masked tokens, the retriever selects potentially useful documents related to the input text, and the model conditions its prediction on both the input and the retrieved documents [2][5].

3. **Training End-to-End to Improve Retrieval and Encoding**: During MLM pre-training, both the retriever and the language model encoder are trained jointly to maximize the marginal likelihood of correctly predicting the masked tokens, marginalizing over the retrieved documents as latent variables. This end-to-end training enables the retriever to learn to identify documents that actually help to reduce prediction uncertainty and helps the encoder incorporate retrieval-augmented context effectively [2][5].

4. **Salient Span Masking Scheme**: REALM uses a masking scheme that focuses on masking salient spans of text rather than random tokens or spans. This scheme ensures that the retrieval module learns to find documents that specifically help in predicting significant missing spans, improving the consistency and strength of the retrieval-learning signal, which is crucial for performance [1].

5. **Improved Knowledge Access and Prediction**: By retrieving relevant documents and conditioning predictions on them, REALM significantly increases the likelihood of predicting correct tokens for masked spans that require external knowledge, as shown in an example where REALM assigns much higher probability to the correct missing token “Fermat” compared to a standard BERT model which does not use retrieval [1].

6. **Fine-Tuning for Open-Domain QA**: After this pre-training, the model can be fine-tuned on Open-QA tasks where the input is a question and the output is an answer. Because the retrieval-augmented encoder already knows how to leverage retrieved documents effectively, it can better access and use the relevant knowledge corpus to answer questions accurately [2][3][5].

Thus, the MLM pre-training objective in REALM improves downstream Open-QA by jointly optimizing
questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM improves downstream open-domain question answering by augmenting masked-language-model (MLM) pre-training with a learned textual knowledge retriever. 

First, during pre-training, REALM employs a masked language modeling task where the model predicts the original value of each masked token in the input sequence [E5]. This MLM pre-training differs slightly from fine-tuning but fundamentally focuses on reconstructing masked inputs [E5].

Second, unlike traditional language models that store knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly integrates a learned textual knowledge retriever component into the pre-training process [E1][E2]. This retriever enables the model to actively retrieve relevant external textual information during pre-training rather than relying purely on encoded parameter knowledge [E1].

Third, by requiring the model to retrieve and incorporate relevant external knowledge in the masked token prediction task, REALM explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge, grounding the model’s predictions in actual retrieved documents instead of just parameter memorization [E1][E2][E3][E4].

However, the provided evidence does not directly describe how this MLM pre-training setup translates to improved downstream open-domain question answering performance, so the exact downstream benefit is not documented in these notes.

An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new

use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(

a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section

Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
